directions.name: Tighten Grouped-Row L2 Feed In The 128x128 PTX Hot Band
directions.hypothesis: Round 46's grouped-row traversal already proved that hot-band block order was the right locality lever: median runtime dropped from 26.892288 ms to 25.677312 ms, became the new best custom run, and moved past the local CUTLASS baseline while tensor active stayed flat at 48.24 -> 48.22. The next win is likely not more raw math scheduling, but a narrower follow-on that keeps the grouped-row mapping and further improves how adjacent CTAs reuse B/A lines and feed shared memory, because dram throughput collapsed to 12.90% and lts throughput recovered to 29.45% while mio_throttle still sits at 4.67%.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual hot-band L2-to-shared feed inefficiency in the dominant 128x128 PTX microkernel, showing up as remaining mio_throttle plus modest shared-bank pressure even after locality improved.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu: kFixedHotBandGroupedRows and the grouped physical_pid -> logical_block_{x,y} remap in bf16_gemm_v1_tensor_core_fixed_hot_band_128x128_ptx_microkernel
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu: stage_b_shared_tile_async and b_shared_col_from_logical for hot-band B staging layout
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu: the hot-band launch path around launch_fixed_hot_band_by_tile_n and the fixed 128x128 PTX microkernel dispatch
directions.risk: Changing CTA traversal or feed order can easily give back the grouped-row locality gain, especially if a more aggressive group size or remap improves L2 reuse for one operand but worsens shared feed cadence or tail behavior. This is the best-ranked path only if the implementation stays narrow and preserves the accepted 128x128 PTX microkernel shape.
metrics_to_recheck: median runtime and TFLOP/s with the user target still set to <20 ms
directions.stop_condition: Stop if a grouped-row/feed tweak pushes dram throughput back up without a runtime drop, or if it regresses the current 25.677312 ms best.

directions.name: Trim PTX Hot-Band Ps2r Live Range Without Losing Occupancy
directions.hypothesis: The PTX hot-band path still reloads the mirrored-column B fragment inside ptx_wmma_accumulate_col_tiles_64x64_ptx_microkernel for every row pair, whereas the 256x128 helper already has a lookahead form that keeps one B fragment live across the sweep. With locality now much better and warps active only slightly improved to 16.86, the next compute-side gain may come from reviving a small ps2r/live-range optimization in the 128x128 PTX path so Tensor Core issue rises without reintroducing the broader accumulator/export lifetime problems that earlier PTX experiments had.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Compute-side fragment feed and shared-to-register pressure inside the hot-band PTX microkernel, contributing to residual mio_throttle and leaving tensor active essentially flat even after the locality fix.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu: the lookahead reference path in ptx_wmma_accumulate_col_tiles_64x64_lookahead for the 256x128 branch
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu: ptx_wmma_store_tile_pairs_64x64_ptx_microkernel and FixedHotBandTile128x128PtxExportScratch if the live-range trim needs to stay balanced with export scratch pressure
directions.risk: This path can quietly trade ps2r savings for register pressure. If one more B fragment or extra temporaries stay live too long, warps_active can fall, occupancy can get tighter, and the kernel can lose the locality gain it just earned.
metrics_to_recheck: launch__occupancy_limit_registers and any reported registers per thread
directions.stop_condition: Stop if tensor activity does not improve or if warps_active / occupancy regress enough to erase the ps2r benefit.

directions.name: Re-evaluate K16 Versus K32 Stage Cadence After Locality Recovery
directions.hypothesis: Barrier, short scoreboard, and long scoreboard all improved with grouped-row traversal, but barrier is still 7.63%, which means stage cadence is not fully off the table. The repo already contains both the accepted K16 double-buffered PTX microkernel and a K32 staged 128x128x32 kernel. Now that locality is healthier, it is worth re-checking whether a stage cadence change can reduce handoff overhead enough to matter, but only after the feed-side and ps2r candidates above because the current profile no longer says stage is the dominant problem.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Steady-state cp.async handoff cadence and CTA sync frequency in the 128x128 hot band, with risk that a deeper per-stage payload raises shared pressure faster than it reduces barriers.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu: cp_async_commit_group, cp_async_wait_group_0, cp_async_wait_group_1, and the hot-band launch selection logic
directions.risk: This can reopen the exact class of issues that the current run just reduced: more stage payload can raise shared traffic, bank pressure, and barrier cost together. It is worth revisiting, but only as a measured comparison rather than as the first follow-up.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if stage retuning lowers barrier a little but raises shared/feed pressure or total runtime, because the remaining gap to <20 ms is too large to spend many rounds on marginal cadence wins.

notes:
- Context: round 46's grouped-row traversal moved the measured median runtime from 26.892288 ms to 25.677312 ms, set a new best custom run, and already beat the local CUTLASS baseline, but the user goal is still <20 ms so winning the baseline is only a waypoint.
- Hotspot weighting: the dominant profile cost is still the grouped 128x128 PTX hot-band kernel; the peeled 64x384 and tiny tail kernel are much smaller, so diagnosis ranking stays focused on the hot band.
- Headline delta read: tensor active 48.24 -> 48.22 was basically flat, warps active 16.68 -> 16.86 ticked up, barrier 8.48 -> 7.63 fell, short scoreboard 2.03 -> 1.91 fell, and long scoreboard 1.42 -> 1.29 fell. This says the gain came from cleaner feed/locality rather than a new compute schedule.
- Coalescing Access: partially validated already. Grouped-row likely fixed the most important access-order waste, so I am not ranking generic vectorization/coalescing changes first.
- Data Reuse: strongly supported. dram throughput dropping to 12.90% while lts throughput returned to 29.45% is the clearest sign that the grouped-row remap improved cross-CTA reuse, so dir_01 keeps pushing this family.
- Async Copy: already present and functional through cp.async, so the question is not whether to add async copy but whether the cadence and recycle timing are still optimal. That makes Async Copy a lower-priority refinement, not the lead move.
- Bank Conflict: still worth watching. The hot-band kernel shows non-trivial l1tex bank read/write activity, so some of the remaining feed pain may still be hidden as shared-bank friction rather than pure DRAM latency.
- L2 Cache: the current profile most clearly endorses this family. Locality got better without raising tensor active, which is why the top recommendation stays on grouped-row/L2/feed refinement.
- Register Reuse: still a live secondary family. The 128x128 PTX microkernel keeps tight accumulator/export state, but its mirrored-column B reload pattern suggests there is still room to trade a little register reuse for fewer ps2r/shared reloads if occupancy survives.
- Pg2s: no longer the lead suspect. The global-to-shared path is already 16-byte cp.async based, and the dramatic DRAM drop says gross pg2s inefficiency is not the main thing blocking <20 ms.
- Ps2r: still attractive. The PTX hot-band path reloads B fragments per row pair instead of using the lookahead pattern that exists elsewhere, so dir_02 explicitly re-tests this family under the new locality regime.
- Stage: worth re-evaluating, but only third. Barrier improved enough that stage cadence no longer looks like the primary limiter, yet 7.63% barrier is still high enough to justify a controlled K16 vs K32 check after the higher-confidence feed and ps2r ideas.
